name: Cannes Film Festival (80th edition)
tagline: Twelve days when world cinema climbs twenty-four red-carpeted steps and the Croisette becomes the most photographed street on earth.
history: The Festival de Cannes was born as an act of defiance. In 1939, French officials — dismayed that the Venice Film Festival had fallen under fascist influence — planned a rival festival on the Riviera, but Hitler's invasion of Poland cancelled it days before opening night. The festival finally launched in September 1946 in a war-weary but glamour-hungry Cannes, and within a decade it had become cinema's great annual summit. The Palme d'Or, first awarded in 1955, quickly established itself as the most coveted prize in film after the Oscar — arguably above it in artistic prestige.

Cannes has always mirrored the culture around it. In May 1968, Jean-Luc Godard and François Truffaut famously shut the festival down in solidarity with striking students and workers, an episode that led to the creation of the independent Directors' Fortnight sidebar. Over the decades the event grew into a twin-engined machine: the artistic competition inside the Palais des Festivals, and the Marché du Film alongside it — the world's largest film market, where thousands of deals determine what the planet will watch for years to come.

The festival's mythology is stitched from unforgettable moments: Brigitte Bardot posing on the beach in 1953 and inventing the modern photocall; Quentin Tarantino's Pulp Fiction taking the 1994 Palme d'Or to gasps and boos; Bong Joon-ho's Parasite winning in 2019 before sweeping the Oscars, proof of Cannes' power as a launchpad. The 2027 edition is a landmark — the 80th festival, celebrating eight decades of premieres, scandals, discoveries and the nightly ritual of black-tie crowds ascending the red steps as the whole town looks on.
whyGo: Nothing else in Europe concentrates this much cinema, celebrity and Riviera theatre into one walkable seafront town — you can watch stars mount the Palais steps at dusk, catch a free classic on the beach after dark, and swim in the Mediterranean between screenings. Even without a festival badge, the spectacle of Cannes in May is a bucket-list experience, and the 80th-anniversary edition promises an exceptional lineup and retrospectives.

gettingThere.route: Zürich (ZRH) → Nice Côte d'Azur (NCE) direct with SWISS or easyJet, then TER regional train Nice-Ville → Cannes
gettingThere.cost: CHF 120–350 return + ~€5–7 each way for the TER
gettingThere.notes: Book flights 2–3 months out — festival fortnight sends NCE fares climbing. Airport tram/bus 12 min to Nice-Ville station; base in Nice as planned and commute to Cannes by train.

gettingThere.route: Zürich HB → Genève (IC direct) → Marseille/Nice (TGV), change for Cannes; or Zürich HB → Milano Centrale (EC) → Ventimiglia → Cannes along the coast
gettingThere.notes: Scenic but a full day. The Geneva–Nice TGV leg sells out around the festival — book on SBB or SNCF Connect as soon as tickets open (~4 months ahead).

gettingThere.route: Zürich → Gotthard tunnel → Milano → Genova → A8 coastal autoroute → Cannes (~600 km)
gettingThere.cost: ~€90–130 each way (fuel + Swiss vignette + Italian/French tolls)
gettingThere.notes: Avoid bringing the car into Cannes itself during the festival — roads close, parking is scarce and expensive. Park in Nice and train in.

costs.notes: Estimates for flights from Zürich, 3 nights and daily costs. Accommodation is the price driver: Cannes hotel rates triple or quadruple during the festival, which is why basing in Nice (30 min by train) saves hundreds. Public access to the festival itself is largely free (beach screenings, sidebar tickets, people-watching) — splurge budgets reflect Cannes seafront hotels and paid gala/party access.

weather: Mid-May on the Riviera is reliably lovely: 17–22°C days, abundant sunshine, sea just warm enough for the brave, with a small chance of a spring shower. Pack light layers, sunglasses and comfortable shoes for long Croisette days — plus one genuinely smart outfit in case an evening invitation materialises (gala screenings enforce black tie).
tips: You cannot simply buy tickets to competition premieres — official screenings are badge- and invitation-only. If you're 18–28, apply early for the free '3 Days in Cannes' cinephile accreditation on the official site; it's the best-value way in.
The Cinéma de la Plage screens classics free on Macé beach every night of the festival — deckchairs, big screen, stars sometimes introducing the films. Arrive an hour early for a seat.
Directors' Fortnight (Quinzaine des Cinéastes) and ACID sidebar screenings sell tickets to the public and often show the festival's most exciting discoveries — check their separate sites in April.

Base in Nice and commute: TER trains run every 20–30 minutes and cost a few euros, versus Cannes hotels at 3–4x normal rates. Check the last train back (around midnight) before committing to a late screening.
For star-spotting, stake out the Palais steps from late afternoon before the 7pm gala, or linger near the Hôtel Martinez and Carlton entrances — the classic celebrity thoroughfares.
Carry cash-light and book restaurants days ahead; anywhere within two blocks of the Croisette fills with industry dinners. Lunch at the Marché Forville end of town is half the price of the seafront.

localRecs.name: Le Suquet
localRecs.note: Cannes' medieval old town on the hill west of the port — climb to the Musée des Explorations du Monde tower at golden hour for the definitive view over the bay and the festival lights.

localRecs.name: Astoux et Brun
localRecs.note: Institution for oysters and seafood platters near the Forville market since 1953 — no-nonsense, bustling, and far better value than the Croisette terraces.

localRecs.name: Marché Forville
localRecs.note: Covered morning market two streets behind the port: socca, cheeses, Provençal produce — ideal cheap lunch assembly before an afternoon of festival-watching.

localRecs.name: L'Amiral bar, Hôtel Martinez
localRecs.note: The festival's unofficial living room — order one (expensive) cocktail and watch deals, directors and stars drift through the lobby.

localRecs.name: Île Sainte-Marguerite
localRecs.note: 15-minute boat from the old port to pine forests, quiet coves and the fort that held the Man in the Iron Mask — the perfect half-day escape from festival crowds.

photos.caption: Crowds and photographers on the famous red carpet at the Palais des Festivals during the Cannes Film Festival
The Palais des Festivals et des Congrès, home of the festival's competition screenings and the red-carpeted steps
The Boulevard de la Croisette and beachfront, Cannes' palm-lined promenade that becomes the festival's catwalk each May
